task: Add per-tenant rate limiting to the public API without breaking existing clients.
project_summary: Relay is a small HTTP API gateway that forwards requests to internal services. It is used by a handful of paying customers and needs fair-use limits before onboarding more.
current_direction: Introduce rate limiting that is safe to roll out gradually and observable before it is enforced.

decisions.title: Use a token-bucket limiter keyed by tenant
decisions.body: Each tenant gets a refillable bucket; requests over the limit get a 429 with a Retry-After header.
decisions.rationale: Token buckets absorb short bursts while still capping sustained load, which fits real client traffic.
decisions.tradeoffs: Needs shared state across gateway instances, so it depends on the Redis decision below.

evidence.claim: 429 with Retry-After is the standard signal for rate limiting

decisions.title: Store counters in Redis, not in process memory
decisions.body: Counters live in Redis so all gateway instances share one view of a tenant's usage.
decisions.rationale: In-process counters would let a client exceed the limit by spreading requests across instances.

decisions.title: Ship in shadow mode first
decisions.body: Log what would have been limited for two weeks before returning any 429s.
decisions.rationale: Rolling out enforcement blind risks cutting off a paying customer on a wrong threshold.

sources.label: MDN — 429 Too Many Requests
sources.bound_claim: 429 with Retry-After is the standard signal for rate limiting

prompts.text: Given this Redis client, implement a token-bucket rate limiter with a configurable refill rate and burst size. Return remaining tokens and a retry-after value.

constraints: Existing clients must not see behavior change until enforcement is turned on
Every limited request must be observable in metrics before it is enforced
boundaries: Do not change the public request or response schema
Do not enforce limits in the first release; log only
what_to_avoid: In-process counters — they undercount across instances
A fixed-window counter — it allows double-rate bursts at the window edge
open_questions: What is a fair default limit per tenant before customers ask for more?
Should internal service-to-service calls be exempt from limits?
next_steps: Add a shadow-mode limiter that logs would-be 429s with the tenant and threshold
Add a per-tenant metrics dashboard for request and limit counts
definition_of_done: Shadow mode is live and reporting would-be limits per tenant in metrics, with zero change to client-visible responses.

recommended_assistant.reason: The next step is implementing a shadow-mode limiter, so a coding assistant fits.
